subject: Mathematics

unit.unitOverview: Welcome to Unit 6! In this unit you will become confident with money – the coins and notes we use every day to buy the things we need. When Amina helps her mother at the Hargeisa market, when Yusuf saves up his shillings to buy a football, or when Halima counts out coins to pay for anjero and camel milk, they are all using money. By the end of this unit you will understand it too.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, activities and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured from the Cambridge Primary Mathematics 0096 — Stage 2 workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: Recognise the different coins and notes used in shillings and know what each is worth.
Compare the value of two coins or notes and say which is worth more.
Make the same amount of money in different ways using different coins and notes.
Find the fewest coins needed to make a given amount.
Add two or more money amounts together to find a total.
Work out the change you should get back after paying for something.
Solve word problems about shopping, saving and giving in real-life situations.

concepts.title: What Is Money and Why We Use It
concepts.explanation: Money is what we use to buy the things we need or want. Long ago, before money, people had to swap things – a farmer might give a bag of maize to get a chicken. But swapping is hard. What if the person with the chicken did not want maize? Money solves this problem. Everyone agrees that money has value, so you can use it to buy from anyone. The kind of money a country uses is called its currency. In East Africa our currency is the shilling. Kenya uses the Kenyan shilling, and Somalia uses the Somali shilling. Other…
concepts.example: Money comes in two forms. Coins are small, round and made of metal. They are usually used for smaller amounts. Notes (also called banknotes or bills) are made of paper or thin plastic and are used for larger amounts.…

concepts.title: The Coins and Notes We Use
concepts.explanation: Let us meet the money we will use all through this unit. First the coins. Our coins come in these values: 1 sh, 5 sh, 10 sh and 20 sh. The higher the number printed on the coin, the more it is worth. A 20 sh coin is worth the most of our coins, and a 1 sh coin is worth the least. Here are the coins, from smallest value to largest:
concepts.example: One thing surprises many learners: a coin's size does not always tell you its value. A coin can be small but worth a lot, or large but worth only a little. So never judge a coin by how big it is – always read the number…

concepts.title: Comparing the Value of Money
concepts.explanation: To compare two coins or notes means to decide which one is worth more. This is easy once you know the value of each – you simply compare the two numbers, just as you compared numbers in Unit 1. The money with the bigger number is worth more. We can also compare using the symbols you learned in Unit 1. The open, hungry mouth of the symbol always points at the bigger value, like a crocodile that wants the larger meal:
concepts.example: A 20 sh coin > a 10 sh coin (20 is greater than 10).

concepts.title: Different Ways to Make the Same Amount
concepts.explanation: Here is one of the most useful ideas about money: the same amount can be made in many different ways. This is called finding equivalent combinations – different groups of coins that all add up to the same value. A trader does not mind whether you pay 20 sh with one 20 sh coin or with two 10 sh coins, because both are worth exactly 20 sh. Let us find different ways to make 20 sh:

concepts.title: Making an Amount with the Fewest Coins
concepts.explanation: Sometimes you want to pay using as few coins as possible, so your pocket is not heavy with lots of small coins. To do this, follow one simple rule: always start with the biggest coin that fits, then work your way down to smaller coins. Let us make 37 sh using the fewest coins. We start with the biggest coin that is not too much:
concepts.example: Start with a 20 sh coin. Now we have 20 sh, and we still need 17 sh more.

concepts.title: Adding Money to Find a Total
concepts.explanation: Suppose Hassan buys a banana for 25 sh and a mango for 30 sh. How much does he spend altogether? We add: 25 sh + 30 sh = 55 sh. Hassan spends 55 shillings in total.
concepts.example: For larger amounts, it helps to add the tens first and then the ones, just as you learned with place value. To add 45 sh + 32 sh: add the tens, 40 + 30 = 70; add the ones, 5 + 2 = 7; then put them together, 70 + 7 = 77…

explorations.prompt: Write the value of each: a) a coin marked 10 b) a note marked 100 c) a coin marked 5.
explorations.hint: Represent the information, name the rule, then solve one step at a time.

methods.title: How to make an amount with the fewest coins
methods.example: Halima has a coin with 20 printed on it. What is it worth, and is it worth more than a 10 sh coin?
steps: Start with the biggest coin that is not more than the amount.
Take it away from the amount and see what is left.
Add the next biggest coin that fits, and repeat.
Stop when you reach the amount exactly, then add your coins back to check.

methods.title: How to add money amounts
methods.example: Show two different ways to make 30 sh.
steps: Add the tens together first.
Add the ones together next.
Put the tens and ones back together to get the total.

methods.title: How to work out change
methods.example: Make 26 sh using the fewest coins.
steps: Start with the amount you paid.
Subtract the price of the item.
The amount left over is your change (or count up from the price to check).

methods.title: Add a shopping basket
methods.example: Omar buys bread for 15 sh, milk for 30 sh and dates for 25 sh. What is the total?
steps: Solution: Add two at a time.
Omar spends 70 shillings altogether.

methods.title: Work out the change
methods.example: Farah buys a book for 65 sh and pays with a 100 sh note. How much change does she get?
steps: Solution: Change = amount paid − price = 100 − 65 = 35 sh.
Checking by counting up: 65 + 5 = 70, 70 + 30 = 100, and 5 + 30 = 35.
Both ways give 35 sh.

methods.title: Compare two groups
methods.example: Ibrahim has two 20 sh coins. Zahra has one 50 sh note. Who has more money, and by how much?
steps: Solution: Find each total first.
Ibrahim has 20 + 20 = 40 sh.
Zahra has 50 sh.
Since 50 is more than 40, Zahra has more, by 50 − 40 = 10 sh.

workedExamples.title: Name the value
workedExamples.prompt: Halima has a coin with 20 printed on it. What is it worth, and is it worth more than a 10 sh coin?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: The number 20 tells us the coin is worth 20 sh. Since 20 is greater than 10, it is worth more than a 10 sh coin. Always read the printed number to find the value.

workedExamples.title: Make an amount two ways
workedExamples.prompt: Show two different ways to make 30 sh.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: One way is one 20 sh coin and one 10 sh coin (20 + 10 = 30). Another way is three 10 sh coins (10 + 10 + 10 = 30). Both are worth exactly 30 sh, so both are correct.

workedExamples.title: Fewest coins
workedExamples.prompt: Make 26 sh using the fewest coins.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Start big. A 20 sh coin (20), then a 5 sh coin (25), then a 1 sh coin (26). That is three coins: 20 sh, 5 sh and 1 sh. Adding them back, 20 + 5 + 1 = 26, so it is correct.

workedExamples.title: Add a shopping basket
workedExamples.prompt: Omar buys bread for 15 sh, milk for 30 sh and dates for 25 sh. What is the total?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Add two at a time. 15 + 30 = 45, then 45 + 25 = 70 sh. Omar spends 70 shillings altogether.

workedExamples.title: Work out the change
workedExamples.prompt: Farah buys a book for 65 sh and pays with a 100 sh note. How much change does she get?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Change = amount paid − price = 100 − 65 = 35 sh. Checking by counting up: 65 + 5 = 70, 70 + 30 = 100, and 5 + 30 = 35. Both ways give 35 sh.

workedExamples.title: Compare two groups
workedExamples.prompt: Ibrahim has two 20 sh coins. Zahra has one 50 sh note. Who has more money, and by how much?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Find each total first. Ibrahim has 20 + 20 = 40 sh. Zahra has 50 sh. Since 50 is more than 40, Zahra has more, by 50 − 40 = 10 sh.

workedExamples.title: Change with a leftover amount
workedExamples.prompt: Sagal buys a sambusa for 8 sh and pays with a 10 sh coin. What is her change, and which coins might she get?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Change = 10 − 8 = 2 sh. She would get her change as two 1 sh coins (1 + 1 = 2).

workedExamples.title: A two-step problem
workedExamples.prompt: Maryam has 100 sh. She buys mangoes for 40 sh and bananas for 25 sh. How much does she spend, and how much is left?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: First add the prices: 40 + 25 = 65 sh spent. Then subtract from what she had: 100 − 65 = 35 sh left. Two steps: add the costs, then take them away from her money.

workedExamples.title: Work out the change
workedExamples.prompt: Work out the change: a) pay 50 sh for a 40 sh item b) pay 100 sh for a 75 sh item.

workedExamples.title: Practice 10
workedExamples.prompt: Halima has one 20 sh coin, one 10 sh coin and two 5 sh coins. How much does she have?

workedExamples.title: Compare with >, < or =
workedExamples.prompt: Compare with >, < or =: a) 20 sh coin ___ 50 sh note b) two 10 sh coins ___ one 20 sh coin.
workedExamples.solution: a) 20 sh coin < 50 sh note, b) two 10 sh coins = one 20 sh coin.

workedExamples.title: Practice 12
workedExamples.prompt: Find at least five different ways to make 30 sh. List them all.
workedExamples.solution: 1) For example: one 20 sh + one 10 sh; three 10 sh; one 20 sh + two 5 sh; two 10 sh + two 5 sh; six 5 sh (each equals 30 sh).

activities.title: Make Your Own Coins and Notes
activities.materials: paper or cardboard, a pencil, scissors, and bottle caps if you have them.
steps: Cut out small circles for coins and label them 1 sh, 5 sh, 10 sh and 20 sh.
Cut out paper rectangles for notes and label them 50 sh, 100 sh, 200 sh, 500 sh and 1000 sh.
Make several of each so you have plenty to practise with.
Keep your money in a little purse or tin to use in the other activities.
You will use this pretend money all through this unit, so keep it safe.

activities.title: Set Up a Home Shop
activities.materials: your pretend money and a few household items (a mango, a cup, a pencil, a book).
steps: Give each item a price and write a little tag, for example: mango 20 sh, pencil 10 sh, cup 35 sh.
Take turns being the shopkeeper and the customer.
The customer chooses an item and pays. The shopkeeper works out and gives the change.
Check the change together each time. Was it correct?
This is the same shopping game shopkeepers play for real all day long!

activities.title: The Fewest Coins Game
activities.materials: your pretend coins and a partner.
steps: One player calls out an amount, such as 37 sh.
The other player must make that amount using the fewest coins possible.
Count how many coins were used. Can your partner do it with fewer?
Swap roles and play again with a new amount.
Remember the rule: always start with the biggest coin that fits, then go smaller.

activities.title: How Many Ways? (Investigation)
activities.materials: your pretend coins and notes, paper and pencil.
steps: Choose an amount to investigate, such as 50 sh.
Find as many different ways to make it as you can, and write each one down.
Zara thinks there are more than ten ways to make 50 sh. Do you agree?
Share your list with a partner. Did they find any ways you missed?

activities.title: Market Day Role Play (Family Game)
activities.materials: your family, pretend money, and some ‘goods’ to sell.
steps: Set up a pretend market like the one in Mogadishu or Nairobi, with several stalls.
Give everyone some pretend money to start with.
Take turns buying and selling. Sellers must add up totals and give correct change.
At the end, count how much money each person has. Who spent wisely?

activities.title: Saving for Eid (Investigation)
activities.materials: a small tin or jar and your pretend coins.
steps: Imagine you are saving up 100 sh to buy a gift for Eid.
Each ‘day’, add a few coins to your tin and write down the new total.
Keep going until your savings reach 100 sh. How many days did it take?
Show two different combinations of coins and notes that make your 100 sh.

After your activities, talk or write about: Which activity helped you understand giving change best? What was the trickiest part? What would you like more practice with? Share your answer with your teacher at your next session.

rules.title: Value Rule
rules.text: Read the number printed on a coin or note to know its value. Do not judge value by the size of the coin – the number decides.

rules.title: Fewest Coins Rule
rules.text: Always start with the biggest coin that fits, then work down to smaller coins. Check by adding your coins back up to the target amount.

rules.title: Change Rule
rules.text: Change equals the amount you paid minus the price. You can also count up from the price to the amount paid.

terms: The kind of money a country uses
How much a coin or note is worth
The answer when you add amounts
Money you get back when you overpay
Different coins with the same value
A group of coins or notes together
commonMistakes: Judging a coin by its size
Read the number printed on the coin
Thinking there is only one way
Swap big coins for smaller ones that add up the same
Start with the biggest coin that fits
Adding instead of subtracting for change
Subtract the price, or count up to the amount paid

realProblems.prompt: At Eastleigh market, Sumaya buys 3 sambusas that cost 10 sh each. How much does she spend altogether?
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

realProblems.prompt: Liban has 100 sh. He buys a football for 80 sh. How much money does he have left?
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

realProblems.prompt: A shopkeeper in Nairobi sells a mango for 25 sh and a papaya for 40 sh. Noor buys both and pays with a 100 sh note. What is her change?
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

realProblems.prompt: During Eid, Aisha shares 100 sh equally between her two younger brothers. How much does each brother get?
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

realProblems.prompt: Abdi wants to pay exactly 45 sh but has no 20 sh coins. Show one way he can make 45 sh using only 10 sh and 5 sh coins.
realProblems.answer: For example, four 10 sh + one 5 sh (45 sh), or three 10 sh + three 5 sh (45 sh).
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.
realProblems.errorFeedback: For example, four 10 sh + one 5 sh (45 sh), or three 10 sh + three 5 sh (45 sh).

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Find at least five different ways to make 30 sh. List them all.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 1) For example: one 20 sh + one 10 sh; three 10 sh; one 20 sh + two 5 sh; two 10 sh + two 5 sh; six 5 sh (each equals 30 sh).

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Yusuf pays for a 68 sh item with the fewest coins. Which coins does he use, and how many?
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 20+20+20+5+1+1+1 = 68 sh, which is three 20 sh, one 5 sh and three 1 sh coins – 7 coins.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Zara has two 20 sh coins and one 10 sh coin. Omar has one 50 sh note. Who has more, and by how much?
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Zara has 20+20+10 = 50 sh; Omar has 50 sh; they have the same amount (equal).

reasoningPrompts.prompt: You buy bread for 35 sh and milk for 25 sh, and pay with a 100 sh note. How much change do you get?
Explain the key idea in Making an Amount with the Fewest Coins.

To do this, follow one simple rule: always start with the biggest coin that fits, then work your way down to smaller coins. Let us make 37 sh using the fewest coins. reasoningPrompts.prompt: Explain the key idea in Adding Money to Find a Total.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Suppose Hassan buys a banana for 25 sh and a mango for 30 sh. How much does he spend altogether? We add: 25 sh + 30 sh = 55 sh. Hassan spends 55 shillings in total.

questions.question: What does “Currency” mean?
options: The kind of money a country uses
How much a coin or note is worth
The answer when you add amounts
questions.answer: The kind of money a country uses
questions.hint: Use the Unit 6 Math Words & Symbols reference.
questions.explanation: Currency means The kind of money a country uses.

selfAssessment: I can recognise the different coins and notes used in shillings and know what each is worth.
I can compare the value of two coins or notes and say which is worth more.
I can make the same amount of money in different ways using different coins and notes.
I can find the fewest coins needed to make a given amount.
I can add two or more money amounts together to find a total.
I can work out the change you should get back after paying for something.
I can solve word problems about shopping, saving and giving in real-life situations.
